pattern: Decrypting word2 into every possible original string just to check dictionary membership is wasted work, because encryption is a deterministic function: precompute the encryption of every dictionary word once up front, and decrypt(word2) becomes a single hashmap lookup of how many dictionary words happen to encrypt to exactly word2.
hints: encrypt is a simple per-character substitution using a map built from keys/values, and it fails (returns empty) the moment any character of the input isn't in that map.

decrypt could enumerate every possible decoding of word2 (since a 2-character code may map back to more than one key) and check each candidate against the dictionary, but that's exponential in the worst case and unnecessary.

Notice: a candidate original string x is 'a possible decryption of word2 that appears in dictionary' exactly when x is in dictionary AND encrypting x (the same one-way process used by encrypt) produces word2 — encryption is deterministic even though decryption isn't. So in the constructor, encrypt every dictionary word once and count occurrences of each resulting string in a hashmap; decrypt(word2) is then just that hashmap's count for word2 (0 if absent).

edge_inputs.description: statement example: encrypt a word, then decrypt an encrypted string that has two dictionary-valid decodings
encrypting a character not present in keys returns an empty string
a word2 whose second two-character chunk has no matching value at all decrypts to zero, never crashing
an encryptable word2 whose only possible decryption is not in dictionary decrypts to zero
single-character keys/values/dictionary, minimum sizes all around
duplicate values for different keys create multiple valid decryptions of the same code, and every one present in dictionary must be counted
many calls to encrypt and decrypt on the same instance reuse the precomputed dictionary-encryption map correctly each time

stress.description: a small random key alphabet with some duplicate values, a modest dictionary built only from those keys, and many encrypt/decrypt calls
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    import string
    alphabet = list(string.ascii_lowercase[:8])
    rng.shuffle(alphabet)
    keys = alphabet[:6]
    consonants = 'bcdfghjklmnpqrstvwxyz'
    vowels = 'aeiou'
    values = [rng.choice(consonants) + rng.choice(vowels) for _ in keys]
    dictionary = set()
    while len(dictionary) < 12:
        length = rng.randint(1, 6)
        word = ''.join(rng.choice(keys) for _ in range(length))
        dictionary.add(word)
    dictionary = list(dictionary)
    ops = ['Encrypter']
    args = [[keys, values, dictionary]]
    for _ in range(max(1, size)):
        if rng.random() < 0.5:
            length = rng.randint(1, 6)
            word1 = ''.join(rng.choice(keys) for _ in range(length))
            ops.append('encrypt'); args.append([word1])
        else:
            n_chunks = rng.randint(1, 4)
            word2 = ''.join(rng.choice(values) for _ in range(n_chunks))
            ops.append('decrypt'); args.append([word2])
    return [ops, args]
stress.note: a 6-key alphabet with some duplicate 2-letter codes keeps the oracle's per-chunk branching small (bounded decode tree) while still exercising multi-way decryption

complexity.time: O(n) build, O(word length) per call
